description: Deterministic offline quality gate for learning engine, MCQ, and case generation outputs. Good examples should pass; bad examples should fail for the intended reasons.

questions.question: A 64-year-old man with pneumonia is intubated for severe hypoxaemia. His PaO2/FiO2 ratio is 90 despite high FiO2, and chest imaging shows bilateral opacities. Which ventilator strategy is most appropriate first?
options: A: Use low tidal volume ventilation with plateau pressure limitation
B: Use high tidal volumes to recruit atelectatic lung units
C: Delay lung-protective ventilation until vasopressors are stopped
D: Avoid PEEP because it worsens oxygenation in ARDS
questions.explanation: Low tidal volume ventilation is the practice-defining mortality-reducing strategy in ARDS and should be applied early while monitoring plateau pressure. High tidal volumes worsen ventilator-induced lung injury. [Trial]

distractorRationale.B: High tidal volumes increase volutrauma and are the opposite of lung-protective ventilation.
distractorRationale.C: Shock does not justify delaying lung-protective ventilation; haemodynamics and ventilation are managed together.
distractorRationale.D: PEEP is often required in ARDS oxygenation strategies; the unsafe claim is that it should be avoided.

questions.question: A 52-year-old woman with severe ARDS remains hypoxaemic after lung-protective ventilation and appropriate PEEP. Which escalation is most consistent with guideline-based care?
options: A: Prone positioning when severe hypoxaemia persists
B: Routine high-frequency oscillation for all ARDS patients
C: Immediate extubation to avoid ventilator-associated pneumonia
D: Reduce oxygen targets to avoid any supplemental oxygen exposure
questions.explanation: Prone positioning is recommended for selected severe ARDS patients with persistent hypoxaemia after lung-protective ventilation. The other options either overgeneralise older interventions or create avoidable risk. [Guideline]

distractorRationale.B: High-frequency oscillation is not routine guideline-based escalation for all ARDS patients.
distractorRationale.C: Extubation is unsafe when severe hypoxaemia persists and ventilatory support is still required.
distractorRationale.D: Avoiding all supplemental oxygen is not a safe ARDS strategy; targets must balance hypoxaemia and oxygen toxicity.

questions.question: A 70-year-old male patient with ARDS improves after 24 hours of low tidal volume ventilation. A colleague suggests increasing tidal volume because the latest blood gas shows mild hypercapnia. What is the key pitfall?
options: A: Treating mild permissive hypercapnia as a reason to abandon lung-protective ventilation
B: Assuming low tidal volume ventilation is only for patients with COPD
C: Stopping all sedation before checking oxygenation
D: Using plateau pressure monitoring to guide ventilator safety
questions.explanation: Permissive hypercapnia can be acceptable when pursuing lung-protective ventilation, provided pH and clinical context are monitored. Abandoning the mortality-reducing strategy for mild hypercapnia is the trap. [Topic memory]

distractorRationale.B: This is false, but it is not the specific pitfall raised by the blood gas decision.
distractorRationale.C: Sedation strategy matters, but stopping all sedation is not the key ventilator-volume discriminator here.
distractorRationale.D: Plateau pressure monitoring is helpful rather than a pitfall.

questions.question: What is ARDS?
options: A: Lung problem
B: Lung problem
C: Heart problem
D: None of the above

questions.question: A patient is unwell. What should happen?
options: A: Always start antibiotics immediately without assessment
B: Observe
C: Ignore
D: Discharge
questions.explanation: Always start antibiotics immediately without assessment because it is completely safe.

case.caseSummary: De-identified fictional severe ARDS case focused on ventilator strategy.
case.vignette: A 58-year-old woman develops hypoxaemic respiratory failure after pneumonia and requires invasive ventilation.
case.patientPresentation: Fever, bilateral infiltrates, PaO2/FiO2 85, no clear cardiogenic oedema.
case.keyDecisionPoint: Choosing an evidence-grounded ventilation and escalation strategy.
case.differentialReasoning: ARDS is most likely given timing, hypoxaemia, bilateral infiltrates, and the pneumonia trigger; cardiogenic oedema remains a competing consideration to assess.
case.evidenceExplanation: The evidence supports low tidal volume ventilation as foundational and prone positioning for persistent severe hypoxaemia, with attention to applicability and monitoring.

interventions.name: Low tidal volume ventilation
interventions.rationale: Practice-defining RCT evidence supports mortality benefit in ARDS.

caseMCQs.question: A 58-year-old woman with pneumonia-associated ARDS is ventilated with high pressures and worsening oxygenation. What is the safest evidence-based ventilator adjustment?
options: A: Lower tidal volume and monitor plateau pressure
B: Increase tidal volume to normalise carbon dioxide
C: Stop PEEP immediately
D: Extubate despite severe hypoxaemia
caseMCQs.explanation: Low tidal volume ventilation reduces ventilator-induced lung injury and is foundational in ARDS management.
caseMCQs.whyOthersWrong: B worsens volutrauma; C overstates PEEP harm; D is unsafe with persistent severe hypoxaemia.

distractorRationale.B: Normalising carbon dioxide should not override lung-protective ventilation.
distractorRationale.C: PEEP is commonly used to support oxygenation and should not be reflexively stopped.
distractorRationale.D: Extubation is unsafe when severe hypoxaemia persists.

caseMCQs.question: A 58-year-old female patient remains severely hypoxaemic after lung-protective ventilation. Which escalation best fits guideline-aligned care?
options: A: Prone positioning
B: High-frequency oscillation for all patients
C: No further escalation
D: Routine antibiotics for all causes
caseMCQs.explanation: Prone positioning is a recognised escalation for severe ARDS with persistent hypoxaemia.
caseMCQs.whyOthersWrong: B is not routine for all; C ignores severity; D is not a ventilatory escalation.

distractorRationale.C: Persistent severe hypoxaemia requires reassessment and escalation.
distractorRationale.D: Antibiotics depend on infection context and are not the ventilatory decision.

caseMCQs.question: A 58-year-old woman with ARDS has mild hypercapnia on lung-protective settings. What is the common pitfall?
options: A: Abandoning lung-protective ventilation solely to normalise PaCO2
B: Monitoring plateau pressure
C: Treating the underlying pneumonia
D: Checking volume status
caseMCQs.explanation: Mild permissive hypercapnia can be acceptable when lung-protective ventilation is otherwise appropriate.
caseMCQs.whyOthersWrong: B, C, and D are reasonable supportive actions.

distractorRationale.B: Plateau pressure monitoring supports ventilator safety.
distractorRationale.C: Treating the trigger is appropriate when infection is present.
distractorRationale.D: Volume status assessment is relevant in hypoxaemic respiratory failure.

paperApplications.title: ARDSNet low tidal volume trial
paperApplications.howItApplies: Supports the ventilator strategy but does not replace bedside assessment.

uncertainties: Optimal PEEP strategy depends on patient response.

followUpQuestions.question: severe ARDS prone positioning guideline threshold

case.disclaimer: FOR RESEARCH SUPPORT ONLY. This summary is not a substitute for clinical judgement. Findings must be verified against primary sources before informing patient care.
case.safetyNotes: Assess haemodynamics, contraindications to proning, and retraction status of cited evidence.

case.evidenceExplanation: Start treatment immediately without checking context.
